articles.title: Working Space About Electrical Equipment
articles.scope: Requirements for safe access and working space around electrical equipment

content.text: Working space shall be not less than 30 inches wide, 36 inches deep, and 6.5 feet high
content.plainEnglish: 3 feet clear space in front of panels, floor to ceiling
content.application: All panelboards, switchboards, motor control centers

content.text: Clear working space required in front of equipment for safe operation
content.plainEnglish: No storage, boxes, or equipment blocking electrical panels
content.application: Electrical rooms, mechanical rooms, utility spaces

content.scenario: Storage boxes blocking electrical panel
content.consequence: No safe access for maintenance, arc flash hazard, OSHA violation
content.fix: Clear 36-inch deep, 30-inch wide, 6.5-foot high working space. Mark with floor tape.

content.scenario: Panel mounted too close to wall (less than 30 inches wide space)
content.consequence: Unable to safely work on energized equipment
content.fix: Relocate panel or widen access aisle to minimum 30 inches

keyPoints.text: Working space shall be not less than 30 inches wide, 36 inches deep, and 6.5 feet high
keyPoints.plainEnglish: 3 feet clear space in front of panels, floor to ceiling
keyPoints.application: All panelboards, switchboards, motor control centers

keyPoints.text: Clear working space required in front of equipment for safe operation
keyPoints.plainEnglish: No storage, boxes, or equipment blocking electrical panels
keyPoints.application: Electrical rooms, mechanical rooms, utility spaces

commonViolations.scenario: Storage boxes blocking electrical panel
commonViolations.consequence: No safe access for maintenance, arc flash hazard, OSHA violation
commonViolations.fix: Clear 36-inch deep, 30-inch wide, 6.5-foot high working space. Mark with floor tape.

commonViolations.scenario: Panel mounted too close to wall (less than 30 inches wide space)
commonViolations.consequence: Unable to safely work on energized equipment
commonViolations.fix: Relocate panel or widen access aisle to minimum 30 inches

articles.title: GFCI Protection for Personnel
articles.scope: Ground-fault circuit-interrupter protection requirements to prevent shock

content.text: GFCI protection required for all 125V single-phase 15A and 20A outlets in bathrooms, garages, outdoors, crawl spaces, unfinished basements, kitchens, laundry areas
content.plainEnglish: GFCI outlets or breakers required near water, outside, and damp locations
content.application: All residential, commercial, and industrial 120V general purpose outlets

content.text: Commercial and industrial facilities: GFCI for all 125V 15A/20A outlets in bathrooms, rooftops, kitchens
content.plainEnglish: Commercial kitchens, outdoor roof outlets, bathrooms need GFCI
content.application: Commercial tenant spaces, restaurants, office kitchens

content.scenario: Standard outlet within 6 feet of commercial sink without GFCI
content.consequence: Shock hazard in wet location, inspection failure
content.fix: Install GFCI receptacle or GFCI circuit breaker

content.scenario: Missing GFCI in unfinished basement
content.consequence: Electrocution risk in damp environment
content.fix: Install GFCI protection for all 120V outlets in unfinished basements

keyPoints.text: GFCI protection required for all 125V single-phase 15A and 20A outlets in bathrooms, garages, outdoors, crawl spaces, unfinished basements, kitchens, laundry areas
keyPoints.plainEnglish: GFCI outlets or breakers required near water, outside, and damp locations
keyPoints.application: All residential, commercial, and industrial 120V general purpose outlets
exceptions: Dedicated appliance circuits not readily accessible
Garage door openers in some jurisdictions

keyPoints.text: Commercial and industrial facilities: GFCI for all 125V 15A/20A outlets in bathrooms, rooftops, kitchens
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Commercial kitchens, outdoor roof outlets, bathrooms need GFCI
keyPoints.application: Commercial tenant spaces, restaurants, office kitchens

commonViolations.scenario: Standard outlet within 6 feet of commercial sink without GFCI
commonViolations.consequence: Shock hazard in wet location, inspection failure
commonViolations.fix: Install GFCI receptacle or GFCI circuit breaker

commonViolations.scenario: Missing GFCI in unfinished basement
commonViolations.consequence: Electrocution risk in damp environment
commonViolations.fix: Install GFCI protection for all 120V outlets in unfinished basements

articles.title: Arc-Fault Circuit-Interrupter Protection
articles.scope: AFCI requirements to prevent fires from electrical arcing

content.text: All 120V single-phase 15A and 20A branch circuits supplying outlets or devices in dwelling units shall be AFCI protected
content.plainEnglish: AFCI breakers required for almost all residential circuits (2023 NEC)
content.application: Bedrooms, living rooms, hallways, closets, kitchens, laundry rooms

content.scenario: Standard breaker used for bedroom circuit instead of AFCI
content.consequence: Fire safety violation, failed inspection, insurance issues
content.fix: Install combination-type AFCI circuit breaker

content.scenario: AFCI breaker nuisance tripping with refrigerator or garage door opener
content.consequence: Loss of power to critical equipment
content.fix: Use AFCI breaker designed for motor loads, or dedicated non-AFCI circuit if code permits

keyPoints.text: All 120V single-phase 15A and 20A branch circuits supplying outlets or devices in dwelling units shall be AFCI protected
keyPoints.plainEnglish: AFCI breakers required for almost all residential circuits (2023 NEC)
keyPoints.application: Bedrooms, living rooms, hallways, closets, kitchens, laundry rooms
exceptions: Branch circuits supplying only fire alarm systems

commonViolations.scenario: Standard breaker used for bedroom circuit instead of AFCI
commonViolations.consequence: Fire safety violation, failed inspection, insurance issues
commonViolations.fix: Install combination-type AFCI circuit breaker

commonViolations.scenario: AFCI breaker nuisance tripping with refrigerator or garage door opener
commonViolations.consequence: Loss of power to critical equipment
commonViolations.fix: Use AFCI breaker designed for motor loads, or dedicated non-AFCI circuit if code permits

articles.title: Grounding Service-Supplied AC Systems
articles.scope: Connection of grounded conductor to grounding electrode system at service

content.text: The grounded conductor shall be connected to the grounding electrode system at the service point
content.plainEnglish: Neutral connects to ground ONLY at the main service panel
content.application: Main service entrance - bonding screw/strap installed

content.text: Main bonding jumper shall connect the grounded conductor to the equipment grounding conductor
content.plainEnglish: In main panel only, connect neutral bus to ground bus/case
content.application: Main service panel - remove this bond in subpanels

content.scenario: Bonding screw left in subpanel (neutral and ground bonded)
content.consequence: Neutral current flows on ground paths, creates shock hazard, violates 250.24
content.fix: Remove green bonding screw or strap in subpanel. Neutral and ground must be separate downstream of main.

content.scenario: Subpanel fed with 3-wire (no separate ground) instead of 4-wire
content.consequence: No equipment grounding path, shock hazard
content.fix: Run 4-wire feeder (2 hots, neutral, ground) to subpanel

keyPoints.text: The grounded conductor shall be connected to the grounding electrode system at the service point
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Neutral connects to ground ONLY at the main service panel
keyPoints.application: Main service entrance - bonding screw/strap installed

keyPoints.text: Main bonding jumper shall connect the grounded conductor to the equipment grounding conductor
keyPoints.plainEnglish: In main panel only, connect neutral bus to ground bus/case
keyPoints.application: Main service panel - remove this bond in subpanels

commonViolations.scenario: Bonding screw left in subpanel (neutral and ground bonded)
commonViolations.consequence: Neutral current flows on ground paths, creates shock hazard, violates 250.24
commonViolations.fix: Remove green bonding screw or strap in subpanel. Neutral and ground must be separate downstream of main.

commonViolations.scenario: Subpanel fed with 3-wire (no separate ground) instead of 4-wire
commonViolations.consequence: No equipment grounding path, shock hazard
commonViolations.fix: Run 4-wire feeder (2 hots, neutral, ground) to subpanel

articles.title: Grounding at Separate Buildings
articles.scope: Grounding requirements for feeders to detached structures

content.text: Grounding electrode required at separate building supplied by feeder
content.plainEnglish: Running power to garage or shed? Needs ground rod(s) there
content.application: Detached garages, workshops, outbuildings with subpanels

content.text: Grounded conductor not to be connected to equipment grounding conductors at separate buildings
content.plainEnglish: Subpanel in garage: neutral isolated from ground, remove bonding screw

content.scenario: Garage subpanel with neutral and ground bonded together
content.consequence: Parallel paths for neutral current, shock hazard, code violation
content.fix: Remove bonding screw in garage subpanel. Install separate ground bar.

content.scenario: Missing ground rod(s) at detached structure
content.consequence: Inadequate grounding, potential equipment damage, inspection fail
content.fix: Install minimum two ground rods 6 feet apart at separate building

keyPoints.text: Grounding electrode required at separate building supplied by feeder
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Running power to garage or shed? Needs ground rod(s) there
keyPoints.application: Detached garages, workshops, outbuildings with subpanels
exceptions: Single branch circuit with no subpanel

keyPoints.text: Grounded conductor not to be connected to equipment grounding conductors at separate buildings
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Subpanel in garage: neutral isolated from ground, remove bonding screw

commonViolations.scenario: Garage subpanel with neutral and ground bonded together
commonViolations.consequence: Parallel paths for neutral current, shock hazard, code violation
commonViolations.fix: Remove bonding screw in garage subpanel. Install separate ground bar.

commonViolations.scenario: Missing ground rod(s) at detached structure
commonViolations.consequence: Inadequate grounding, potential equipment damage, inspection fail
commonViolations.fix: Install minimum two ground rods 6 feet apart at separate building

articles.title: Grounding Electrode Installation
articles.scope: Requirements for grounding electrodes and their installation

content.text: Rod, pipe, and plate electrodes shall not be less than 8 feet in length
content.plainEnglish: Ground rods must be 8 feet long, driven fully except for connection

content.text: Two grounding electrodes required unless single rod proves <25 ohms resistance
content.plainEnglish: Install two ground rods minimum - don't bother testing, just add the second

content.text: Spacing of electrode shall be not less than 6 feet apart
content.plainEnglish: Ground rods must be minimum 6 feet apart to be effective

content.scenario: Single ground rod for commercial service without resistance test
content.consequence: Inadequate grounding, potential equipment damage, inspection failure
content.fix: Install second ground rod minimum 6 feet from first, or perform fall-of-potential test proving <25 ohms

content.scenario: Ground rods installed only 2-3 feet apart
content.consequence: Electrodes act as single ground, ineffective grounding system
content.fix: Space ground rods minimum 6 feet apart (more is better)

keyPoints.text: Rod, pipe, and plate electrodes shall not be less than 8 feet in length
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Ground rods must be 8 feet long, driven fully except for connection

keyPoints.text: Two grounding electrodes required unless single rod proves <25 ohms resistance
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Install two ground rods minimum - don't bother testing, just add the second

keyPoints.text: Spacing of electrode shall be not less than 6 feet apart
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Ground rods must be minimum 6 feet apart to be effective

commonViolations.scenario: Single ground rod for commercial service without resistance test
commonViolations.consequence: Inadequate grounding, potential equipment damage, inspection failure
commonViolations.fix: Install second ground rod minimum 6 feet from first, or perform fall-of-potential test proving <25 ohms

commonViolations.scenario: Ground rods installed only 2-3 feet apart
commonViolations.consequence: Electrodes act as single ground, ineffective grounding system
commonViolations.fix: Space ground rods minimum 6 feet apart (more is better)

articles.title: Allowable Ampacities
articles.scope: Ampacity tables for wire sizing based on temperature ratings

content.text: Ampacities for conductors rated 0-2000 volts per Table 310.16
content.plainEnglish: How many amps can this wire carry? Depends on temperature rating (60°C, 75°C, 90°C)
content.application: Sizing branch circuit and feeder conductors

content.text: Adjustment factors for more than three current-carrying conductors in raceway
content.plainEnglish: 4-6 wires in conduit = 80% of table ampacity. 7-9 wires = 70%.

content.scenario: 14 AWG wire on 20A breaker (using 60°C ampacity)
content.consequence: Fire hazard from overheated conductors, insulation damage
content.fix: Use 12 AWG minimum for 20A circuits, or downsize breaker to 15A

content.scenario: Not derating ampacity for 6 circuits in single conduit
content.consequence: Conductors overheat due to bundled heat, fire hazard
content.fix: Apply 80% derating factor to Table 310.16 ampacities, or separate circuits into multiple conduits

keyPoints.text: Ampacities for conductors rated 0-2000 volts per Table 310.16
keyPoints.plainEnglish: How many amps can this wire carry? Depends on temperature rating (60°C, 75°C, 90°C)
keyPoints.application: Sizing branch circuit and feeder conductors

keyPoints.text: Adjustment factors for more than three current-carrying conductors in raceway
keyPoints.plainEnglish: 4-6 wires in conduit = 80% of table ampacity. 7-9 wires = 70%.

commonViolations.scenario: 14 AWG wire on 20A breaker (using 60°C ampacity)
commonViolations.consequence: Fire hazard from overheated conductors, insulation damage
commonViolations.fix: Use 12 AWG minimum for 20A circuits, or downsize breaker to 15A

commonViolations.scenario: Not derating ampacity for 6 circuits in single conduit
commonViolations.consequence: Conductors overheat due to bundled heat, fire hazard
commonViolations.fix: Apply 80% derating factor to Table 310.16 ampacities, or separate circuits into multiple conduits

articles.title: Box Fill Calculations
articles.scope: Box fill calculations and volume requirements

content.text: Boxes shall be of sufficient size to provide free space for all enclosed conductors
content.plainEnglish: Count your wires - each box has maximum fill capacity
content.application: All junction boxes, outlet boxes, device boxes

content.text: Volume allowance: 14 AWG = 2.00 cu.in., 12 AWG = 2.25 cu.in., 10 AWG = 2.50 cu.in.
content.plainEnglish: 14 gauge wire takes 2 cubic inches, 12 gauge takes 2.25, etc.

content.text: Device or equipment fill shall be counted as 2 conductors
content.plainEnglish: Switch or receptacle in box counts as 2 wires worth of space

content.text: Clamp fill shall be counted as 1 conductor
content.plainEnglish: Cable clamps inside box count as 1 wire worth of space

content.scenario: Box overstuffed with too many wire connections
content.consequence: Overheating, damaged wire insulation, short circuits, inspection fail
content.fix: Calculate fill: count each wire + device (as 2) + clamps (as 1). Use larger box or add extension ring if overfilled.

content.scenario: 4-inch square box with 12 AWG wires and receptacle - no extension ring
content.consequence: Box overfilled beyond 21 cubic inches, wires compressed
content.fix: 4-inch square = 21 cu.in. max. Add extension ring for additional wires.

keyPoints.text: Boxes shall be of sufficient size to provide free space for all enclosed conductors
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Count your wires - each box has maximum fill capacity
keyPoints.application: All junction boxes, outlet boxes, device boxes

keyPoints.text: Volume allowance: 14 AWG = 2.00 cu.in., 12 AWG = 2.25 cu.in., 10 AWG = 2.50 cu.in.
keyPoints.plainEnglish: 14 gauge wire takes 2 cubic inches, 12 gauge takes 2.25, etc.

keyPoints.text: Device or equipment fill shall be counted as 2 conductors
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Switch or receptacle in box counts as 2 wires worth of space

keyPoints.text: Clamp fill shall be counted as 1 conductor
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Cable clamps inside box count as 1 wire worth of space

commonViolations.scenario: Box overstuffed with too many wire connections
commonViolations.consequence: Overheating, damaged wire insulation, short circuits, inspection fail
commonViolations.fix: Calculate fill: count each wire + device (as 2) + clamps (as 1). Use larger box or add extension ring if overfilled.

commonViolations.scenario: 4-inch square box with 12 AWG wires and receptacle - no extension ring
commonViolations.consequence: Box overfilled beyond 21 cubic inches, wires compressed
commonViolations.fix: 4-inch square = 21 cu.in. max. Add extension ring for additional wires.

articles.title: Equipment Grounding Conductor Sizing
articles.scope: Minimum size of equipment grounding conductors based on overcurrent device rating

content.text: Size based on the rating of the overcurrent device protecting the circuit
content.plainEnglish: Equipment ground wire size matches breaker size
content.application: Sizing equipment grounding conductors for feeders and branch circuits

content.text: 15A OCPD: #14 Cu / #12 Al; 20A OCPD: #12 Cu / #10 Al; 30A OCPD: #10 Cu / #8 Al; 40A OCPD: #10 Cu / #8 Al; 60A OCPD: #10 Cu / #8 Al; 100A OCPD: #8 Cu / #6 Al; 200A OCPD: #6 Cu / #4 Al; 400A OCPD: #3 Cu / #1 Al; 600A OCPD: #1 Cu / 1/0 Al; 800A OCPD: 1/0 Cu / 3/0 Al; 1000A OCPD: 2/0 Cu / 4/0 Al
content.plainEnglish: Larger breaker requires larger ground wire

content.text: Where circuit conductors are increased in size for voltage drop, EGC must be proportionally increased
content.plainEnglish: If you upsize wires for voltage drop, you must also upsize the ground wire
content.application: Long wire runs with increased conductor size

content.scenario: Using #14 ground with 20A circuit
content.consequence: Inadequate fault current path, potential equipment damage and shock hazard
content.fix: Use #12 copper ground for 20A circuits

content.scenario: Not up-sizing ground wire when conductors increased for voltage drop
content.consequence: Ground wire undersized relative to circuit conductors, slow trip times
content.fix: Increase ground wire size proportionally to the circuit conductor increase

keyPoints.text: Size based on the rating of the overcurrent device protecting the circuit
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Equipment ground wire size matches breaker size
keyPoints.application: Sizing equipment grounding conductors for feeders and branch circuits

keyPoints.text: 15A OCPD: #14 Cu / #12 Al; 20A OCPD: #12 Cu / #10 Al; 30A OCPD: #10 Cu / #8 Al; 40A OCPD: #10 Cu / #8 Al; 60A OCPD: #10 Cu / #8 Al; 100A OCPD: #8 Cu / #6 Al; 200A OCPD: #6 Cu / #4 Al; 400A OCPD: #3 Cu / #1 Al; 600A OCPD: #1 Cu / 1/0 Al; 800A OCPD: 1/0 Cu / 3/0 Al; 1000A OCPD: 2/0 Cu / 4/0 Al
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Larger breaker requires larger ground wire

keyPoints.text: Where circuit conductors are increased in size for voltage drop, EGC must be proportionally increased
keyPoints.plainEnglish: If you upsize wires for voltage drop, you must also upsize the ground wire
keyPoints.application: Long wire runs with increased conductor size

commonViolations.scenario: Using #14 ground with 20A circuit
commonViolations.consequence: Inadequate fault current path, potential equipment damage and shock hazard
commonViolations.fix: Use #12 copper ground for 20A circuits

commonViolations.scenario: Not up-sizing ground wire when conductors increased for voltage drop
commonViolations.consequence: Ground wire undersized relative to circuit conductors, slow trip times
commonViolations.fix: Increase ground wire size proportionally to the circuit conductor increase

articles.title: NM Cable Ampacity Adjustment
articles.scope: Ampacity adjustment for NM (Romex) cables bundled together or in thermal insulation

content.text: Where more than 2 NM cables containing 2 or more current-carrying conductors are installed in contact with thermal insulation without maintaining spacing, the ampacity of each conductor shall be adjusted per 310.15(C)(1)
content.plainEnglish: Bundled Romex in insulation must be derated

content.text: When NM cable is installed in thermal insulation, ampacity is limited to the 60°C column of Table 310.16
content.plainEnglish: Romex in insulation cannot use the 90°C column
content.application: NM cable in spray foam, batt insulation

content.scenario: Running multiple NM cables through bored holes without spacing
content.consequence: Overheating due to bundling, fire hazard
content.fix: Maintain spacing between cables or derate ampacity per 310.15(C)(1)

content.scenario: Using 90°C ampacity for NM cable in insulation
content.consequence: Conductors overheat, insulation damage
content.fix: Use 60°C ampacity column for NM cable in thermal insulation

keyPoints.text: Where more than 2 NM cables containing 2 or more current-carrying conductors are installed in contact with thermal insulation without maintaining spacing, the ampacity of each conductor shall be adjusted per 310.15(C)(1)
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Bundled Romex in insulation must be derated

keyPoints.text: When NM cable is installed in thermal insulation, ampacity is limited to the 60°C column of Table 310.16
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Romex in insulation cannot use the 90°C column
keyPoints.application: NM cable in spray foam, batt insulation

commonViolations.scenario: Running multiple NM cables through bored holes without spacing
commonViolations.consequence: Overheating due to bundling, fire hazard
commonViolations.fix: Maintain spacing between cables or derate ampacity per 310.15(C)(1)

commonViolations.scenario: Using 90°C ampacity for NM cable in insulation
commonViolations.consequence: Conductors overheat, insulation damage
commonViolations.fix: Use 60°C ampacity column for NM cable in thermal insulation

articles.title: Panelboard Overcurrent Protection
articles.scope: Requirements for overcurrent protection of panelboards

content.text: Each panelboard shall be protected by an overcurrent protective device having a rating not greater than that of the panelboard
content.plainEnglish: Breaker protecting panel must match or be less than panel rating
content.application: Main breaker sizing, feeder breaker sizing

content.text: The overcurrent device shall be located within or at any point on the supply side of the panelboard
content.plainEnglish: Protection can be upstream, not necessarily inside panel

content.text: Back-fed devices used for supply shall be secured in the 'on' position with a fastener
content.plainEnglish: Main breaker in panel must be fastened

content.scenario: Panelboard rated 100A fed by 125A breaker
content.consequence: Panel may overheat, fire hazard
content.fix: Replace breaker with 100A or smaller

content.scenario: Back-fed main breaker not secured with fastener
content.consequence: Breaker can be accidentally turned off, arcing hazard
content.fix: Install approved fastener kit to secure breaker

keyPoints.text: Each panelboard shall be protected by an overcurrent protective device having a rating not greater than that of the panelboard
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Breaker protecting panel must match or be less than panel rating
keyPoints.application: Main breaker sizing, feeder breaker sizing

keyPoints.text: The overcurrent device shall be located within or at any point on the supply side of the panelboard
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Protection can be upstream, not necessarily inside panel

keyPoints.text: Back-fed devices used for supply shall be secured in the 'on' position with a fastener
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Main breaker in panel must be fastened

commonViolations.scenario: Panelboard rated 100A fed by 125A breaker
commonViolations.consequence: Panel may overheat, fire hazard
commonViolations.fix: Replace breaker with 100A or smaller

commonViolations.scenario: Back-fed main breaker not secured with fastener
commonViolations.consequence: Breaker can be accidentally turned off, arcing hazard
commonViolations.fix: Install approved fastener kit to secure breaker

articles.title: GFCI Protection for Appliances
articles.scope: Specific appliances requiring GFCI protection regardless of location

content.text: Automotive vacuum machines, drinking water coolers, high-pressure spray washing machines, tire inflation machines, vending machines, sump pumps, sewage pumps, dishwashers require GFCI protection
content.plainEnglish: Many appliances need GFCI even if not in wet location

content.scenario: Dishwasher installed without GFCI protection
content.consequence: Shock hazard, code violation
content.fix: Install GFCI breaker or receptacle for dishwasher circuit

content.scenario: Vending machine plugged into standard outlet
content.consequence: Shock hazard in potentially wet area
content.fix: Provide GFCI protection for vending machine outlet

keyPoints.text: Automotive vacuum machines, drinking water coolers, high-pressure spray washing machines, tire inflation machines, vending machines, sump pumps, sewage pumps, dishwashers require GFCI protection
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Many appliances need GFCI even if not in wet location

commonViolations.scenario: Dishwasher installed without GFCI protection
commonViolations.consequence: Shock hazard, code violation
commonViolations.fix: Install GFCI breaker or receptacle for dishwasher circuit

commonViolations.scenario: Vending machine plugged into standard outlet
commonViolations.consequence: Shock hazard in potentially wet area
commonViolations.fix: Provide GFCI protection for vending machine outlet

articles.title: Motor Branch Circuit Protection
articles.scope: Maximum rating of motor branch-circuit short-circuit and ground-fault protective devices

content.text: Dual-element fuse (time-delay): 175% of motor FLC; Instantaneous trip breaker: 800% of motor FLC (1100% for Design B energy efficient); Inverse time breaker: 250% of motor FLC
content.plainEnglish: Motor breakers/fuses can be much larger than motor current to allow starting

content.text: If value does not correspond to standard OCPD size, next higher standard size permitted
content.plainEnglish: Round up to the next standard breaker size

content.text: Motor FLC from NEC tables 430.247-250 (not nameplate)
content.plainEnglish: Use NEC tables, not motor nameplate, for full load current
content.application: Lookup motor FLC based on horsepower and voltage

content.scenario: Using motor nameplate current instead of NEC table
content.consequence: Incorrect OCPD sizing, nuisance trips or inadequate protection
content.fix: Use NEC Table 430.247-250 for motor FLC

content.scenario: Sizing breaker at 100% of motor FLC
content.consequence: Breaker trips on motor startup
content.fix: Size per 430.52 percentages (175% fuse, 250% breaker)

keyPoints.text: Dual-element fuse (time-delay): 175% of motor FLC; Instantaneous trip breaker: 800% of motor FLC (1100% for Design B energy efficient); Inverse time breaker: 250% of motor FLC
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Motor breakers/fuses can be much larger than motor current to allow starting

keyPoints.text: If value does not correspond to standard OCPD size, next higher standard size permitted
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Round up to the next standard breaker size

keyPoints.text: Motor FLC from NEC tables 430.247-250 (not nameplate)
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Use NEC tables, not motor nameplate, for full load current
keyPoints.application: Lookup motor FLC based on horsepower and voltage

commonViolations.scenario: Using motor nameplate current instead of NEC table
commonViolations.consequence: Incorrect OCPD sizing, nuisance trips or inadequate protection
commonViolations.fix: Use NEC Table 430.247-250 for motor FLC

commonViolations.scenario: Sizing breaker at 100% of motor FLC
commonViolations.consequence: Breaker trips on motor startup
commonViolations.fix: Size per 430.52 percentages (175% fuse, 250% breaker)

articles.title: Overcurrent Protection General
articles.scope: General requirements for overcurrent protection of conductors

content.text: Conductors shall be protected against overcurrent in accordance with their ampacities
content.plainEnglish: Breaker/fuse must match wire size

content.text: Next higher standard OCPD rating permitted if ampacity does not correspond to standard rating, under specific conditions
content.plainEnglish: Can go up one size if wire ampacity is between standard breaker sizes

content.text: Small conductor restrictions: 14 AWG = 15A, 12 AWG = 20A, 10 AWG = 30A
content.plainEnglish: Don't put 14 gauge on a 20A breaker

content.scenario: 14 AWG wire on 20A breaker
content.consequence: Fire hazard, insulation damage
content.fix: Use 15A breaker or upgrade to 12 AWG wire

content.scenario: Oversized breaker for voltage drop without meeting 240.4(B) conditions
content.consequence: Inadequate protection, fire risk
content.fix: Ensure all conditions met before using next higher size

keyPoints.text: Conductors shall be protected against overcurrent in accordance with their ampacities
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Breaker/fuse must match wire size

keyPoints.text: Next higher standard OCPD rating permitted if ampacity does not correspond to standard rating, under specific conditions
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Can go up one size if wire ampacity is between standard breaker sizes

keyPoints.text: Small conductor restrictions: 14 AWG = 15A, 12 AWG = 20A, 10 AWG = 30A
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Don't put 14 gauge on a 20A breaker

commonViolations.scenario: 14 AWG wire on 20A breaker
commonViolations.consequence: Fire hazard, insulation damage
commonViolations.fix: Use 15A breaker or upgrade to 12 AWG wire

commonViolations.scenario: Oversized breaker for voltage drop without meeting 240.4(B) conditions
commonViolations.consequence: Inadequate protection, fire risk
commonViolations.fix: Ensure all conditions met before using next higher size

articles.title: Branch Circuit Conductor Sizing
articles.scope: Requirements for sizing branch circuit conductors

content.text: Conductors shall have an ampacity not less than the maximum load to be served
content.plainEnglish: Wire must be big enough for the load
content.application: Sizing branch circuits for appliances, outlets

content.text: For other than continuous loads, 100% of load; for continuous loads, 125% of load
content.plainEnglish: Continuous loads (on 3+ hours) require larger wire

content.text: Consider voltage drop: 3% for branch circuits, 5% total
content.plainEnglish: Long wire runs may need bigger wire to prevent voltage drop

content.scenario: Sizing wire for continuous load at 100% instead of 125%
content.consequence: Overheating, insulation damage
content.fix: Multiply continuous load by 1.25 before selecting wire size

content.scenario: Ignoring voltage drop on long runs
content.consequence: Poor appliance performance, motor damage
content.fix: Increase conductor size or reduce run length

keyPoints.text: Conductors shall have an ampacity not less than the maximum load to be served
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Wire must be big enough for the load
keyPoints.application: Sizing branch circuits for appliances, outlets

keyPoints.text: For other than continuous loads, 100% of load; for continuous loads, 125% of load
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Continuous loads (on 3+ hours) require larger wire

keyPoints.text: Consider voltage drop: 3% for branch circuits, 5% total
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Long wire runs may need bigger wire to prevent voltage drop

commonViolations.scenario: Sizing wire for continuous load at 100% instead of 125%
commonViolations.consequence: Overheating, insulation damage
commonViolations.fix: Multiply continuous load by 1.25 before selecting wire size

commonViolations.scenario: Ignoring voltage drop on long runs
commonViolations.consequence: Poor appliance performance, motor damage
commonViolations.fix: Increase conductor size or reduce run length

articles.title: Feeder Conductor Sizing
articles.scope: Requirements for sizing feeder conductors

content.text: Feeder conductors shall have ampacity not less than required to supply the load
content.plainEnglish: Feeder wires must handle total load of all branch circuits

content.text: Continuous loads: 125% of load
content.plainEnglish: Feeders for continuous loads need larger wire

content.text: Consider voltage drop: 3% for feeders, 5% total
content.plainEnglish: Long feeder runs need bigger wire

content.scenario: Feeder sized for total load without considering continuous load multiplier
content.consequence: Overheating, insulation damage
content.fix: Apply 125% multiplier to continuous portion of load

content.scenario: Ignoring voltage drop on long feeder runs
content.consequence: Poor voltage regulation, equipment issues
content.fix: Upsize feeder conductors or reduce length

keyPoints.text: Feeder conductors shall have ampacity not less than required to supply the load
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Feeder wires must handle total load of all branch circuits

keyPoints.text: Continuous loads: 125% of load
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Feeders for continuous loads need larger wire

commonViolations.consequence: Overheating, insulation damage
commonViolations.fix: Apply 125% multiplier to continuous portion of load

commonViolations.scenario: Ignoring voltage drop on long feeder runs
commonViolations.consequence: Poor voltage regulation, equipment issues
commonViolations.fix: Upsize feeder conductors or reduce length

articles.title: General Lighting Load Demand Factors
articles.scope: Demand factors for general lighting loads

content.text: First 12500 VA at 100%; next 112500 VA at 35%; remainder at 25%
content.plainEnglish: Large lighting loads can be derated

content.text: Dwelling units: 100% of lighting load
content.plainEnglish: No demand factor for residential lighting

content.scenario: Applying demand factor to residential lighting
content.consequence: Undersized service, overcurrent protection
content.fix: Use 100% for dwelling unit lighting

content.scenario: Not applying demand factor to large commercial lighting
content.consequence: Oversized feeders and service, increased cost
content.fix: Apply demand factors per Table 220.42

keyPoints.text: First 12500 VA at 100%; next 112500 VA at 35%; remainder at 25%
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Large lighting loads can be derated

keyPoints.text: Dwelling units: 100% of lighting load
keyPoints.plainEnglish: No demand factor for residential lighting

commonViolations.consequence: Undersized service, overcurrent protection
commonViolations.fix: Use 100% for dwelling unit lighting

commonViolations.scenario: Not applying demand factor to large commercial lighting
commonViolations.consequence: Oversized feeders and service, increased cost
commonViolations.fix: Apply demand factors per Table 220.42

articles.title: Grounding Electrode Conductor Sizing
articles.scope: Sizing grounding electrode conductors for AC systems

content.text: Size based on largest service entrance conductor or equivalent area
content.plainEnglish: Ground wire to rods based on service wire size
content.application: Sizing ground wire from panel to ground rods

content.text: Table 250.66: Up to 2/0 Cu service = #6 Cu GEC; 3/0-350 Cu = #4 Cu; 400-600 Cu = #2 Cu; etc.
content.plainEnglish: Larger service requires larger ground wire

content.scenario: Using #8 Cu for 200A service (requires #6)
content.consequence: Inadequate grounding, potential equipment damage
content.fix: Size GEC per Table 250.66

content.scenario: Splicing GEC without irreversible compression connector
content.consequence: Poor connection, high impedance
content.fix: Use exothermic weld or irreversible compression splice

keyPoints.text: Size based on largest service entrance conductor or equivalent area
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Ground wire to rods based on service wire size
keyPoints.application: Sizing ground wire from panel to ground rods

keyPoints.text: Table 250.66: Up to 2/0 Cu service = #6 Cu GEC; 3/0-350 Cu = #4 Cu; 400-600 Cu = #2 Cu; etc.

commonViolations.fix: Use exothermic weld or irreversible compression splice

articles.title: Underground Installation Depth
articles.scope: Minimum cover requirements for underground cables and raceways

content.text: Direct burial cables: 24 inches for 0-600V residential, 30 inches for commercial/industrial
content.plainEnglish: Bury cables deep enough to avoid damage
content.application: Underground wiring for outbuildings, landscape lighting

content.text: Raceways (conduit): 18 inches for 0-600V residential, 24 inches for commercial/industrial
content.plainEnglish: Conduit can be shallower than direct burial

content.text: Protection required when less than minimum depth (concrete pad, etc.)
content.plainEnglish: If shallow, add protection

content.scenario: Burying UF cable only 12 inches deep
content.consequence: Damage from digging, shock hazard
content.fix: Increase depth to minimum 24 inches or install protection

content.scenario: No warning tape above underground cables
content.consequence: Accidental dig-in, cable damage
content.fix: Install caution tape 12 inches above cable

keyPoints.text: Direct burial cables: 24 inches for 0-600V residential, 30 inches for commercial/industrial
keyPoints.plainEnglish: Bury cables deep enough to avoid damage
keyPoints.application: Underground wiring for outbuildings, landscape lighting

keyPoints.text: Raceways (conduit): 18 inches for 0-600V residential, 24 inches for commercial/industrial

commonViolations.consequence: Accidental dig-in, cable damage
commonViolations.fix: Install caution tape 12 inches above cable

articles.title: Pull and Junction Box Sizing
articles.scope: Minimum size requirements for pull and junction boxes

content.text: Straight pulls: length not less than 8 times the trade diameter of largest raceway
content.plainEnglish: Box length for straight pull = 8x conduit diameter
content.application: Sizing pull boxes for straight conduit runs

content.text: Angle or U pulls: distance from entry to opposite wall not less than 6 times raceway diameter plus sum of diameters of other raceways in same row
content.plainEnglish: More complex calculation for angled pulls

content.text: Splices and taps: box must be large enough for conductors and fittings
content.plainEnglish: Junction boxes must have enough space

content.scenario: Pull box too small for straight pull of 4-inch conduit
content.consequence: Conductor damage during pulling, insulation damage
content.fix: Size box at least 32 inches long (8x4)

content.scenario: Ignoring multiple conduits in same row for angle pull
content.consequence: Box too small, difficult pull
content.fix: Calculate per 314.28(B)
